coredata.dc:title: Highly sensitive determination of dopamine by a turn-on fluorescent biosensor based on aptamer labeled carbon dots and nano-graphite
coredata.dc:creator: Zhu, Ling
Xu, Guanhong
Song, Quan
Tang, Tang
Wang, Xu
Wei, Fangdi
Hu, Qin
coredata.dc:description: In this paper, a novel fluorescent biosensor based on DA aptamer labeled carbon dots (aptamer-CDs) and nano-graphite (NG) for the determination of dopamine (DA) was developed. In this system, aptamer-CDs were in charge of energy donor (fluorophor) and chemical recognition, while NG served as an energy acceptor. In the absence of DA, aptamer-CDs were adsorbed on the surface of NG through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, which led to the fluorescence quenching of aptamer-CDs due to the surface energy transfer. When it was in presence, DA combined with its aptamer anchored on the surface of CDs to form specific globular chain structure which would detach from the surface of NG and thus the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CDs was recovered. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CDs increased linearly with the increase of DA concentration in the range of 0.10–5.00nM and the limit of detection was 0.055nM. This method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in human urine samples with satisfactory results.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Dopamine aptamer
Nano-graphite
Dopamine
Fluorescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of DA aptamer labeled CDs conjugates
Working principle of the as-prepared biosensor for DA detection
Characterization of CDs and DA aptamer labeled-CDs
Characterization of DA aptamer labeled CDs
Determination of DA in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of DA detection strategy of aptamer-CDs/NG biosensor.
Fluorescence emission spectra of aptamer-CDs (a), aptamer-CDs/NG (b) and aptamer-CDs/NG/DA complex (c).
Fluorescence spectrum of aptamer-CDs (a) and absorption spectra of NG (b), aptamer-CDs (c) and aptamer-CDs/NG (d).
Time-resolved fluorescence decay curves of aptamer-CDs (a), aptamer-CDs/NG (b) and aptamer-CDs/NG/DA complex (c).

(A) UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra of CDs. Inset shows the photographs of prepared CDs in PBS under visible light (left) and 365nm UV lamp light (right). (B) TEM images, (C) FT-IR spectrum, (D) fluorescence emission spectrum excited at 355nm of CDs.
Agarose gel electrophoresis of (a) aptamer-CDs, (b) aptamer.
Fluorescence intensity changes (F−F0)/F0 of NG-based biosensor in the presence of various potential interferents. Inset: the enlarged area.

Fluorescence emission spectra of NG-based biosensor in the presence of different concentrations of DA (from a to g: 0, 0.10, 0.20, 0.50, 1.00, 4.00 and 5.00nM). Inset: calibration plot: fluorescence intensity changes [(F- F0)/F0] versus DA (0–5nM).
The intra-day and inter-day precisions of the determination of DA (n=3).
Determination of DA in urine samples (n=3).

head.ce:title: Highly sensitive determination of dopamine by a turn-on fluorescent biosensor based on aptamer labeled carbon dots and nano-graphite

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University, Nanjing, Jiangsu 211166, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this paper, a novel fluorescent biosensor based on DA aptamer labeled carbon dots (aptamer-CDs) and nano-graphite (NG) for the determination of dopamine (DA) was developed. In this system, aptamer-CDs were in charge of energy donor (fluorophor) and chemical recognition, while NG served as an energy acceptor. In the absence of DA, aptamer-CDs were adsorbed on the surface of NG through π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, which led to the fluorescence quenching of aptamer-CDs due to the surface energy transfer. When it was in presence, DA combined with its aptamer anchored on the surface of CDs to form specific globular chain structure which would detach from the surface of NG and thus the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CDs was recovered. Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CDs increased linearly with the increase of DA concentration in the range of 0.10–5.00nM and the limit of detection was 0.055nM. This method was successfully applied to the determination of DA in human urine samples with satisfactory results.

ce:section.ce:para: Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitter in the central nervous system and is a precursor of norepinephrine in the biological pathway [1,2]. It plays an important role in cardiovascular and central nervous system as well as endocrine system [3]. Shortage of DA is correlated with kinds of neurological disorders, such as Alzheimer, schizophrenia and Parkinson’s disease [4,5]. In clinical practice, the level of DA is detected to help the diagnosis of above diseases. Till now, various analytical methods have been applied to detecting DA including high performance liquid chromatography equipped with fluorescence detector or electrochemical detector [6–9], electrochemical assays [10–12], fluorescent spectrometry [13–15], surface-enhanced raman scattering spectroscopy [16–18]. Among these analytical methods, fluorescence assay using nanoparticles as fluorophores, such as semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) and carbon dots (CDs), has drawn more and more attention for its rapid, simple and convenient operations, and nanoparticles’ excellent optical performances [19–23]. However, the fluorescence assay is limited for the lack of the nanoparticles’ selectivity. In order to overcome this drawback, the introduction of aptamer to the fluorescence assay is one of effective ways [24,26].

Aptamers are artificial, single-stranded DNA or RNA sequences which are selected in vitro by systematic evolution of ligands by exponential enrichment [26,27]. They can combine with their homologous targets with high affinity. Based on this property, aptamers have been widely applied to fabricating various biosensors for analyzing of proteins [24,28–30], metal ions [25,31], and other small molecules [32,33].

However, most of the above research was based on turn-off fluorescent probes, which lack high sensitivity due to strong matrix effects. So, a biosensor based on turn-on mechanism is imminently required to improve the sensitivity. In recent years, papers about expanding NG into the system to realize a turn-on mechanism have been reported [27,34].

Nano-graphite (NG) has recently been considered as a promising energy acceptor based on energy transfer mechanism. As NG is a π-rich nanoparticle which is similar to graphene oxide (GO), it can quench lots of fluorescent materials such as organic dye, QDs and other metal nanoclusters. An excellent donor-acceptor pair can be formed through the strong π-π interaction between NG and CDs [27,34]. In comparison with GO, NG is cheaper and easier to prepare. Moreover, as a carbon-based nanomaterial, NG shows good biocompatibility and low cytotoxicity which provides great application prospects in wide fields.

In this study, a novel “turn-on” fluorescent biosensor based on DA aptamer labeled CDs (aptamer-CDs) and NG for highly sensitive determination of DA was developed. Wherein, aptamer-CDs served as the fluorophor and DA recognition group, and NG with π-rich structure was used as a superior nanoquencher. In the absence of DA, aptamer-CDs were adsorbed on the surface of NG which led to the fluorescence quenching of aptamer-CDs due to surface energy transfer (SET). When DA was present, it combined with its aptamer on the surface of CDs to form specific globular chain structure which would detach from the surface of NG and thus the fluorescence intensity of aptamer-CDs was recovered. The conditions of preparing and applying the biosensor were optimized in relevant aspects. Finally, the as-prepared biosensor was successfully applied to the determination of DA in complicated biological fluids.

Citric acid monohydrate, N-(3-dimethylaminopropyl)-N-ethylcarbodiimide hydrochloride (EDC), uric acid (UA) and ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Diethylene triamine and acetone were purchased from Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Dopamine aptamer: 5′-NH2-(CH2)6-GTC TCT GTG TGC GCC AGA GAA CAC TGG GGC AGA TAT GGG CCA GCA CAG AAT GAG GCC-3′ was purchased from Shanghai Sangon Biological Engineering Technology & Services Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The NG (5–15nm) was purchased from Nanjing XFNANO Materials Tech Co., Ltd. (Nanjing, China). Dopamine hydrochloride (DA), l-epinephrine (LEE), l-norepinephrine (NE), 5-hydroxytryptamine (5-HT) and isoprenaline hydrochloride (IPE) were purchased from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 5mM, pH 7.5). The purified water used in the study was prepared using Direct-Q water purification system (Millipore, USA). All reagents were of analytical grade and without any further purification.

FT-IR spectra were recorded on a TENSOR 27 spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a resolution of 2cm−1 and a spectral range of 4000–400cm−1.The fluorescence spectra were recorded with an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). UV–vis absorption spectra were measured with a UV2450 spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopic and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were acquired on a JEOL JEM-2100 (HR) transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan). Fluorescence decay curves were performed with a steady state and transient state fluorescence spectrometer (FM-4P-TCSPC, Horiba Jobin Yvon, France). Aptamer combined on the surface of CDs was confirmed by gel electrophoresis (Shanghai Tanon HE-120) in 2.0% agarose gel with 0.5×TBE buffer (pH 8.0) at 80V for 30min.

CDs were synthesised according to a reported method [35]. Briefly, citric acid monohydrate (1.2g) and diethylene triamine (600μL) were added into 20mL of ultrapure water. Then the mixture was transferred to a Teflon equipped stainless steel autoclave (30mL), which was placed in a drying oven followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200°C for 4h. When cooled down to room temperature, the products were concentrated by rotary evaporation, followed by purification with acetone for three times. The final products were dispersed in ultrapure water as stock solution (8μg/mL) after vacuum drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of DA aptamer labeled CDs conjugates
ce:section.ce:para: The conjugates were prepared by well-established carbodiimide chemistry [36,37]. 30μL of 1mg/μL EDC in 5mM PBS was added to 100μL of CDs solution (8μg/mL) and the mixture was sonicated for 20min. Afterwards, 10μL of the mixture was added to 90μL of 3.3μM DA aptamer in 5mM PBS, and further incubated for 2h at 25°C.

The determination of DA was conducted as follow. 50μL of NG (0.45mg/mL) was added into 100μL of aforementioned aptamer-CDs solution to quench the fluorescence, and then the mixture was incubated for 20min at room temperature. Afterwards, 50μL of DA standard solution was introduced into the reaction system, and the system was incubated for 1h at 20°C. The signal output values were calculated according to the relative fluorescence intensity [(F−F0)/F0], in which F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensities of the proposed based biosensor excited at 355nm in the presence and absence of DA, respectively. It is important to mention that NG suspension should be shaken adequately during the experiment.
The urine samples were collected from two healthy volunteers. 0.1mL of urine was diluted to 6mL with PBS, and the determination of DA in the urine samples was performed as described in Section 2.5.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Working principle of the as-prepared biosensor for DA detection
ce:section.ce:para: The design strategy of the as-prepared biosensor is shown in Scheme 1 . First of all, DA aptamer was modified on the surface of CDs to form a CDs marked biosensor of DA according to the well-established carbodiimide chemistry. In the absence of DA, aptamer-CDs would be adsorbed on the surface of NG via π-π stacking and hydrophobic interaction, the fluorescence of aptamer-CDs was quenched via SET. When in the presence of DA, DA would combine with the aptamer capped on the surface of CDs to form globular chain structures [38]. The complex would detach from the surface of NG, and the fluorescence intensity of DA aptamer capped CDs recovered significantly. The experiment principle is well identified by fluorescent experimental results shown in Fig. 1 .

To investigate the mechanism of the fluorescence quench, related studies were carried out. Fig. 2 showed the fluorescence spectrum of aptamer-CDs (a) and absorption spectra of NG (b), aptamer-CDs (c) and aptamer-CDs mixed with NG (d). As can be seen in Fig. 2a and b, NG had no absorption in the emission wavelength range of aptamer-CDs. The result indicated that quenching of the fluorescence of aptamer-CDs was not caused by inner filter effect. Comparison with Fig. 2c, the absorbance decrease around 350nm (CDs) after the introduction of NG was attributed to the combination between aptamer-CDs and NG (Fig. 2d). NG could combine with CDs through the strong π-π interaction for its π-rich structure, which partially obstructed excited electrons from the conduction band to the valance band and thus partially quenched the fluorescence of aptamer-CDs [39].

To further reveal the mechanism of fluorescence quenching, time-resolved fluorescence spectra of aptamer-CDs, NG based biosensor before and after adding DA were measured. As shown in Fig. 3 , the fluorescence lifetime of the aptamer-CDs remained constant with addition of NG and DA, which implied that the fluorescence quenching of aptamer-CDs by NG might be static quenching [40].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CDs and DA aptamer labeled-CDs

ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b) and emission (c) spectra were recorded to investigate the optical properties of the as-prepared CDs. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the prepared CDs had absorption peaks at 350nm and 240nm, respectively. According to the reference [41], the peak at 240nm is attributed to π-π* transition of aromatic CC bonds, while the absorption at 350nm is attributed to n-π* transition of CO bonds. The maximum excitation wavelength and the maximum emission wavelength of the CDs were recorded to be 355nm and 437nm, respectively. A full width at half maximum of the CDs was around 63nm.

As shown in a typical HRTEM image (Fig. 4B), the as-prepared CDs were well dispersed with a narrow size distribution of 1.5–3.0nm. The inset in Fig. 4B showed the lattice spacing of 0.19nm which corresponds to the [102] facet of graphite [42].

Fig. 4C showed the FT-IR spectrum of the CDs. The absorption peaks at 3248cm−1 and 1558cm−1 were attributed to the stretching vibration and in-plane bending vibration of NH, respectively. The characteristic peak around 1658cm−1 could be attributed to the stretching vibration of CO. The existence of strong absorption peak of OH (3418cm−1) indicated that there were still plenty of carboxy groups on the surface of the CDs.

Furthermore, the stability of the CDs was investigated when excited at the maximum excitation wavelength for a long time (Fig. 4D). The fluorescence intensity of the CDs remained nearly no change, which indicated the CDs had good resistance to photobleaching.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of DA aptamer labeled CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The agarose gel electrophoresis result in Fig. 5 showed that DA aptamer labeled CDs moved slower than the pure aptamer due to the increase of mass-to-charge ratio of DA aptamer labeled CDs after modified with CDs. It indicated that DA aptamer was successfully modified on the surface of the CDs.

The parameters including media pH, aptamer concentration, incubation temperature of the combination between aptamer and CDs, NG concentration, reaction temperature and time of the combination between aptamer-CDs and DA were optimized.

Because CDs are sensitive to pH change, the effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity of CDs was investigated in the range from 1.91 to 9.18 (Fig. S1A). It can be seen that the fluorescence intensity increased with the increase of the pH value in the range of 1.91–6.86, and remained stable while pH was over 6.86. It can be explained that under acidic conditions, the intermolecular hydrogen-bond interaction between amino group and carboxy group on the surface of functionalized CDs would induce CDs agglomeration so that the fluorescence intensity quenched [43]. Hence, the PBS buffer with a pH value of 7.5 was chosen in the work.

Besides, the concentration of aptamer was also studied. As shown in Fig. S1B, the fluorescence intensity of the aptamer-CDs increased with the increasing concentration of the aptamer up to 15μM, then decreased when the concentration of aptamer was over 15μM. The reason that the fluorescence intensity increased with aptamer concentration increased (<15μM) was that aptamer combined with CDs to form an expanded conjugated system,and thus the fluorescence intensity of the system enhanced. When the concentration of aptamer was excessive (>15μM), the fluorescence of the system decreased as the long chain structure of aptamer might block out the fluorescent sites of the biosensor. Thus, 15μM of the aptamer was chosen as the optimal concentration for the biosensor.

The effect of incubating temperature on the combination between CDs and aptamer was investigated (Fig. S1C). It can be seen that the fluorescent intensity increased with the increase of reaction temperature until 25°C, thus 25°C was chosen as the optimal incubation temperature for this reaction.

Fig. S1D described the quenching effect with different concentrations of NG. The fluorescence intensity decreased significantly with the increasing concentration of NG. Whereas the fluorescence intensity cannot be enhanced by the addition of DA when there was too much NG in the system. Therefore, 112.5μgmL−1 of NG was chosen as the optimal concentration.

The optimization of reaction temperature for fluorescence recovery by DA is shown in Fig. S1E. The results showed that the reaction accelerated with the increase reaction temperature. Thus, 20°C was chosen as the optimal incubation temperature as higher temperature would decrease the stability of DA. As shown in Fig. S1F, the fluorescence intensity increased as the reaction proceeded, and then reached a plateau after 30min 1h was chosen as the reaction time for fluorescence recovery.

Biological matrix due to its complexity may cause interference for the determination of DA. The fluorescent response of the biosensor conducted was investigated against other analogues and other common interferents in urine samples (Fig. 6 ). No obvious interfering effects were observed in the presence of 100nM Pb2+ and 1μM of other metal ions (UA, AA, Mg2+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, K+, Na+), 5nM of DA analogues (LEE, NE, 5-HT, IPE) and other interferents (4mM of glucose and 1μM of urea). It indicated that the as-prepared biosensor possessed outstanding selectivity.

Under the optimal conditions, the fluorescence spectra of the as-prepared biosensor with the addition of different concentrations of DA were studied. As demonstrated in Fig. 7 , the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared biosensor showed linear increase with the increase of DA concentration in a range of 0.10–5.00nM. The calibration curve could be expressed by the equation (F−F0)/F0 =0.0638×ln[DA]+0.2192 with the correlation coefficient of 0.9995 (where, F and F0 represented the fluorescence intensities of the biosensor in presence and absence of DA, respectively). The limit of detection (LOD) was evaluated to be 0.055nM, which was defined as 3σ/k (where, σ is the standard deviation of blank measurements (n=10), and k is the slope of calibration graph). As depicted in Table S1, LOD obtained from this method was found to be lower than those of other methods reported. As shown in Table 1 , the intra-day and inter-day relative standard deviations (RSDs) of three different concentrations were all within 10%.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of DA in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the feasibility of the proposed method, the as-prepared biosensor was applied to the direct determination of DA by using the standard addition method. The sample preparation is very simple, just 60-fold-dilution of urine samples. The analytical results are summarized in Table 2 . The recoveries were in the range of 90.0–120.0% with RSDs less than 6%.
The results indicated that the precision and accuracy of the method met the needs of biological sample analysis.

In summary, a novel turn-on fluorescent biosensor based on aptamer labeled CDs and NG was developed for highly sensitive and selective determination of DA. Compared with the reported methods for DA determination, the proposed biosensor has some advantages: (1) The preparation of biosensor based on aptamer-CDs and NG is simple, rapid and convenient. (2) The biosensor has high sensitivity and good selectivity. (3) All the materials used in the study are low toxic or even nontoxic which avoids environmental pollution. The biosensor built can be applied to the determination DA in biological samples with satisfactory results, indicating its promising future in disease diagnosis.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 81173016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.03.084.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following are Supplementary data to this article:

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Ling Zhu is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of carbon dots for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2013.

Guanhong Xu is an associate professor at School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. He obtained his M.S. on Medicinal chemistry from Nanjing Medical University in 2009. He has been studying for his Ph.D. at the same university since 2012, where his main research interest is in the synthesis and application of carbon dots.

Quan Song is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. He was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2014.

Tang Tang is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of molecularly imprinted stir bar sorptive extraction technology for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2014.
Xu Wang is a graduate student focusing on synthesis and application of carbon dots for M.S. at Nanjing Medical University. She was awarded a B.S. degree in pharmacy from the same university in 2014.

Fangdi Wei is an associate professor at School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She obtained her M.S. on Analytical Chemistry from Nanjing University in 2002. She has been studying for her Ph.D. at Nanjing Medical University since 2009, where her main research interests are in two areas: (1) synthesis and application of surface molecular imprinting polymer and (2) analysis of trace components in life system.

Qin Hu is a professor at School of Pharmacy, Nanjing Medical University. She obtained her Ph.D. on Analytical Chemistry from East China Normal University in 2001. She is currently conducting research in two areas: (1) synthesis of nano-silica molecular imprinted polymer and its application in analysis of environmental endocrine disrupting chemicals and (2) molecular imprinted quantum dots application in bioanalysis.
